title: Running without namespace mapping as non-root
body:
/kind feature

**Description**

There is a use-case where I want to basically run a container as non-root and have no uid or gid mapping. I cannot figure out the correct way to do this. Whenever I use the --user option it will do namespace mapping.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. podman run -it --rm --privileged --user=$(id -u):$(id -g) --volume="/etc/passwd:/etc/passwd:ro" --volume="/etc/group:/etc/group:ro" --volume=$PWD:$PWD" --workdir="$PWD" container_name bash

2. touch some_nonexistent_filename

3. exit

**Describe the results you received:**

ls -l some_nonexistent_filename

file has a uid/gid that was mapped to not be user uid/gid.

**Describe the results you expected:**

ls -l some_nonexistent_filename

file uid/gid that is exactly user uid/gid.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
podman version 1.0.0
```

**Output of `podman info`:**

```
host:
  BuildahVersion: 1.6-dev
  Conmon:
    package: podman-1.0.0-1.git82e8011.fc29.x86_64
    path: /usr/libexec/podman/conmon
    version: 'conmon version 1.12.0-dev, commit: 49780a1cf10d572edc4e1ea3b8a8429ce391d47d'
  Distribution:
    distribution: fedora
    version: "29"
  MemFree: 7128797184
  MemTotal: 33742376960
  OCIRuntime:
    package: runc-1.0.0-66.dev.gitbbb17ef.fc29.x86_64
    path: /usr/bin/runc
    version: |-
      runc version 1.0.0-rc6+dev
      commit: ead425507b6ba28278ef71ad06582df97f2d5b5f
      spec: 1.0.1-dev
  SwapFree: 16834621440
  SwapTotal: 16852709376
  arch: amd64
  cpus: 16
  hostname: desk.mcneil.com
  kernel: 4.19.15-300.fc29.x86_64
  os: linux
  rootless: true
  uptime: 23h 52m 12.77s (Approximately 0.96 days)
insecure registries:
  registries: []
registries:
  registries:
  - docker.io
  - registry.fedoraproject.org
  - quay.io
  - registry.access.redhat.com
  - registry.centos.org
store:
  ConfigFile: /home/sean/.config/containers/storage.conf
  ContainerStore:
    number: 0
  GraphDriverName: overlay
  GraphOptions:
  - overlay.mount_program=/usr/bin/fuse-overlayfs
  GraphRoot: /home/sean/.local/share/containers/storage
  GraphStatus:
    Backing Filesystem: extfs
    Native Overlay Diff: "false"
    Supports d_type: "true"
  ImageStore:
    number: 0
  RunRoot: /run/user/1000
```

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

/etc/subuid has
user:165536:65536

/etc/subgid has
user:165536:65536

comments.body:
I believe that user namespaces are a requirement for rootless as of now. We
can do several things inside them that we can't normally (one that I'm
aware of is mounting tmpfs filesystems).

On Fri, Jan 18, 2019, 02:02 seanmcneil3 <notifications@github.com wrote:

> /kind feature
>
> *Description*
>
> There is a use-case where I want to basically run a container as non-root
> and have no uid or gid mapping. I cannot figure out the correct way to do
> this. Whenever I use the --user option it will do namespace mapping.
>
> *Steps to reproduce the issue:*
>
>    1.
>
>    podman run -it --rm --privileged --user=$(id -u):$(id -g)
>    --volume="/etc/passwd:/etc/passwd:ro" --volume="/etc/group:/etc/group:ro"
>    --volume=$PWD:$PWD" --workdir="$PWD" container_name bash
>    2.
>
>    touch some_nonexistent_filename
>    3.
>
>    exit
>
> *Describe the results you received:*
>
> ls -l some_nonexistent_filename
>
> file has a uid/gid that was mapped to not be user uid/gid.
>
> *Describe the results you expected:*
>
> ls -l some_nonexistent_filename
>
> file uid/gid that is exactly user uid/gid.
>
> *Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only
> occasionally):*
>
> *Output of podman version:*
>
> podman version 1.0.0
>
> *Output of podman info:*
>
> host:

>   Distribution:

>     path: /usr/bin/runc
>     version: |-
>       runc version 1.0.0-rc6+dev
>       commit: ead425507b6ba28278ef71ad06582df97f2d5b5f

I'm not sure we have a mechanism to do this.  @seanmcneil3 The issue is we need to be root inside of a user namespace to mount the container.

@giuseppe What do you think of the idea where we would map 
0:STARTUID:STARTUID+MYUID-1
MYUID:MYUID:1
0:STARTUID+MYUID+1:ENDUID-MYUID

For example
for my account UID=3267

cat /etc/subuid
dwalsh:100000:65536

0:10000:3267
3267:3267:1
3268:103268:62269 (65536-3267)
If we added a mechanism to do this, then you could run a container as your UID, within a non root system.
This could cause issues with the labelling of content in your homedir though.
@rhatdan We are already mapping the user's own UID in as root in the rootless container, AFAIK - @giuseppe can you confirm?
@seanmcneil3 Try dropping the --user - root in the rootless container should already be mapped to your user

@mheon this is the point of the discussion. In the container, uid=0(root) gid=0(root) is mapped to my user. aka uid=1000(sean) gid=1000(sean). What adding --user= option should do is to change my uid/gid to that set, but it should not cause mapping. This is how docker behaves.

The reasoning behind this is that I want to run a virtual environment for my tools. They don't work under current fedora, so I need a virtual centos:6 or ubuntu:12.04 to run things.

Tools or applications running in a container might wish to know and record information about a user. They may access something like what the unix id command does to get the username of the person running the tools. Without using the --user= option, inside the container the username running the tools is "root". This is not what I desire. I want the username to be me: sean. So I pass in the /etc/passwd and /etc/group and use --user=sean:sean.

I don't see this as any security issue as I can basically do anything inside a container anyway and without the --user= option it treats me as root which has full control.

What I'd like to do is be able to user --user= and tell it not to map to namespace.

@giuseppe Any thoughts here? We could alter the mappings, potentially, move root somewhere in the middle of the range of allocated UIDs for rootless?

This would need to be an opt-in, though.

Sorry, it just dawned on me what the security issue is here:

If I don't map root to sean and in the container I have it setup to be able to do something like sudo, then I could read/write as root even if I don't have access as root from original account.

So I guess what I'd like is something as mentioned by @rhatdan where I can just map sean to sean and have everything else go to subuid/subgid.
@seanmcneil3 For a rootless container, we will never map root into UID 0 - and even if we did, you wouldn't have permissions to do anything with root-owned files.

I see. So if I'm understanding things correctly, right now the mapping is happening as:

0 -> users uid
others -> users subuid range

I'm guessing what I'd like when you use the --user=uid:gid is:

uid -> users uid
0 -> users uid
others -> users subuid range

I think we probably want to take the subid range and split it, inserting your UID somewhere in the middle, where it will appear as the actual UID in question. Root will go to the first ID in the subuid range.

you can create another namespace inside the user namespace created by podman.

This is what https://github.com/debarshiray/fedora-toolbox/blob/master/fedora-toolbox#L191-L193 does.

It creates another namespace where the uid 0 (which on the host is mapped to your rootless UID), is mapped back to its original value.

So basically:


|Host|userns created by Podman|userns inside the container|
| ------------- |:-------------:| -----:|
|UID|0|UID|
|OTHER_UIDS|1-N |[0 - UID-1]; [UID+1, N]

I have some part of a workaround based on this. Can get the uid/gid mapping I want:

GID=$(id -g)

podman run -it --rm --privileged --user=$UID:$GID \
 --uidmap $UID:0:1 --uidmap 0:1:$UID \
 --gidmap $GID:0:1 --gidmap 0:1:$GID \
...

with docker, I could even support sudo inside the container, but I'm not sure I really need that. Most of the time I used it to install additional packages to verify when I found a dependency.
@seanmcneil3 could this be closed or is there anything more to sort out?

There is this method of essentially mapping 2 times to get the desired affect. It would be a nice enhancement, however, to be able to disable namespace mapping. I thought --userns=host should do that.
there is no way for an unprivileged user to create a container without a user namespace first.  We wouldn't be able even to use something like `pivot_root` or `chroot` to get into the new rootfs.
